analogy: Think of "The Philosophy of the Constitution" through the chapter's own opening images: 220 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Ten THE PHILOSOPHY OF THE CONSTITUTION INTRODUCTION In this book, so far we have studied some important provisions of our Constitution and the way in which these have worked in the last 69 years. We also studied the way... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: THE PHILOSOPHY OF, THE CONSTITUTION, INTRODUCTION, WHAT IS MEANT BY PHILOSOPHY OF THE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
The Philosophy of the Constitution is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 220 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Ten THE PHILOSOPHY OF THE CONSTITUTION INTRODUCTION In this book, so far we have studied some important provisions of our Constitution and the way in which these have worked in the last 69 years.

The text explains that we also studied the way in which the Constitution was made.

The text explains that but have you ever asked yourself why leaders of the national movement felt the need to adopt a constitution after achieving independence from British rule?

The text explains that why did they choose to bind themselves and the future generations to a constitution?

The text explains that in this book, you have repeatedly visited the debates in the Constituent Assembly.

The text explains that but it should be asked why the study of the constitution must be accompanied by a deep examination of the debates in the Constituent Assembly?

The text explains that this question will be addressed in this chapter.

The text explains that secondly, it is important to ask what kind of a constitution we have given ourselves.

The text explains that what objectives did we hope to achieve by it?

The text explains that what are the strengths and limitations of this vision and, by implication, the achievements and weaknesses of the Constitution?

The text explains that in doing so, we try to understand what can be called the philosophy of the Constitution.

The text explains that after reading this chapter, you should be able to understand: ± why it is important to study the philosophy of the Constitution; ± what are the core features of the Indian Constitution; ± what are the criticisms of this Constitution; and ± what are the limitations of the Constitution?

The text explains that 221 Chapter 10: The Philosophy of the Constitution WHAT IS MEANT BY PHILOSOPHY OF THE CONSTITUTION?

The text explains that some people believe that a constitution merely consists of laws and that laws are one thing, values and morality, quite another.

The text explains that therefore, we can have only a legalistic, not a political philosophy approach to the Constitution.

The text explains that it is true that all laws do not have a moral content, but many laws are closely connected to our deeply held values.

The text explains that we must therefore, look upon the constitution as a document that is based on a certain moral vision.

The text explains that we need to adopt a political philosophy approach to the constitution.

The text explains that what do we mean by a political philosophy approach to the constitution?

The text explains that we have three things in mind. ± First, we need to understand the conceptual structure of the constitution.

The text explains that it means that we must ask questions like what are the possible meanings of terms used in the constitution such as ‘rights’, ‘citizenship’, ‘minority’ or ‘democracy’? ± Furthermore, we must attempt to work out a coherent vision of society and polity conditional upon an interpretation of the key concepts of the constitution.

The text explains that we must have a better grasp of the set of ideals embedded in the constitution. ± Our final point is that the Indian Constitution must be read in conjunction with the Constituent Assembly Debates in order to refine and raise to a higher theoretical plane, the justification of values embedded in the Constitution.

The text explains that when the framers of the Constitution Does it mean that all constitutions have a philosophy?

The text explains that or is it that only some constitutions have a philosophy?

The text explains that 222 Indian Constitution at Work chose to guide Indian society and polity by a set of values, there must have been a corresponding set of reasons.

The text explains that many of them, though, may not have been fully explained.

The text explains that a political philosophy approach to the constitution is needed not only to find out the moral content expressed in it and to evaluate its claims but possibly to use it to arbitrate between varying interpretations of the many core values in our polity.

The text explains that we must, therefore, examine whether or not a serious disjunction exists between the constitutional ideal and its expression in other arenas.

The text explains that since the expression The Japanese Constitution of 1947 is popularly known as the ‘peace constitution’.

The text explains that the philosophy of the Japanese constitution is thus based on the ideal of peace.

The text explains that article 9 of the Japanese constitution states — 1) Aspiring sincerely to an international peace based on justice and order, the Japanese people forever renounce war as a sovereign right of the nation and the threat or use of force as means of settling international disputes.

The text explains that 2) In order to accomplish the aim of the preceding paragraph, land, sea, and air forces, as well as other war potential, will never be maintained… This shows how the context of making the constitution dominates the thinking of the constitution makers.

The text explains that yes, of course, I do remember this issue of different interpretations of the Constitution.

The text explains that we discussed it in the last chapter, didn’t we?

The text explains that 223 Chapter 10: The Philosophy of the Constitution of the ideal in the constitution has considerable authority it must be used to arbitrate in conflict of interpretation over values or ideals.

The text explains that our Constitution can perform this job of arbitration.

The text explains that constitution as Means of Democratic Transformation In the first chapter we have studied the meaning of the term constitution and the need to have a constitution.

The text explains that it is widely agreed that one reason for having constitutions is the need to restrict the exercise of power.

The text explains that constitutions provide these basic rules and therefore, prevent states from turning tyrannical.

The text explains that if you were writing the Constitution today, what provisions would you recommend for remedying this limitation?

The text explains that do you agree with the statement that “it is not clear why in a poor developing country, certain basic socio-economic rights were relegated to the section on Directive Principles rather than made an integral feature of our Fundamental Rights”?

The text explains that what do you think are the possible reasons for putting socio-economic rights in the section on Directive Principles?

The text explains that how did your school celebrate the Constitution Day on November 26th?

The text explains that rEQUEST FOR FEEDBACK How did you like this textbook?

The text explains that what was your experience in reading or using this?

The text explains that what changes would you like to see in the next version of this book?

The text explains that write to us on all these and any other matter related to this textbook.

The text explains that you could be a teacher, a parent, a student or just a general reader.

The text explains that please write to: Coordinator (Political Science) Department of Education in Social Sciences (DESS) National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) Sri Aurobindo Marg, New Delhi 110 016.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 10: The Philosophy of the Constitution

schemes.name: After
schemes.point: After all, the infamous Rowlatt Act, which the national movement opposed so vehemently, sought to deny this basic freedom. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch10 pg 6)

schemes.name: The Constitution
schemes.point: The Constitution also made it possible for the government to reserve public sector jobs for these groups. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch10 pg 7)

schemes.name: Such
schemes.point: Such institutions may receive money from the government. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch10 pg 8)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The state must not intervene in the domain of religion; religion likewise should not dictate state policy or influence the conduct of the state. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch10 pg 9)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The constitutional commitment to caste-based affirmative action programme shows how much ahead India was compared to other nations. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch10 pg 11)

prelims_traps: Do not treat THE PHILOSOPHY OF and THE CONSTITUTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to The Philosophy of the Constitution; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain The Philosophy of the Constitution through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use THE PHILOSOPHY OF and THE CONSTITUTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
